steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Mulder and Spellman concluded that invasive species (L. densiflorum) are advantaged by delays in the onset of subfreezing temperatures. To most directly support this conclusion, a finding must show that L. densiflorum benefits more than the native O. secunda when subfreezing temperatures arrive late.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'L. densiflorum stopped producing leaves sooner than O. secunda did in years with late subfreezing temperature onset': This would show the invasive being disadvantaged relative to the native when temperatures are delayed, which contradicts the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the extension was much greater for L. densiflorum than for O. secunda': Both species extend leaf production when subfreezing temperatures are delayed, but the invasive extends production far more than the native. This directly shows the invasive gaining a greater advantage from the delay, supporting the conclusion. ✓
  • Choice C — 'the years with late subfreezing temperature onset also had early growing season onset in spring': This introduces an additional variable (early spring onset) that could explain the increased leaf production rather than the delayed subfreezing temperatures alone. It muddies the causal link rather than supporting it. ✗
  • Choice D — 'neither L. densiflorum nor O. secunda showed any significant interannual variation in the cessation of leaf production': If neither species changes its leaf production in response to subfreezing temperature variation, that would undermine the conclusion entirely, not support it. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
